notes: Today is Saturday 2026-05-16, US markets are closed. The figures use Friday May 15's confirmed close. Sources varied: Yahoo Finance and Morningstar report $422.24 (-4.75%) while GuruFocus reports $424.45 (-4.8%) — a $2.21 gap that just exceeds the $2 cross-check threshold, so the lower figure is used per policy.

news.sentiment_summary: Sentiment turned sharply cautious heading into the weekend after Friday's -4.75% slide wiped out a week of gains. The trigger was the Trump-Xi Beijing summit ending with no China Full Self-Driving (FSD) regulatory breakthrough — a development bulls had been positioning for all week. Layered on top: Tesla's CFO confirmed the company will burn cash for the rest of 2026 due to a record $25 billion capital spending plan, and US regulators forced a recall of 218,868 vehicles for a rearview-camera software glitch. The bullish robotaxi story is still alive — the unsupervised fleet has more than quadrupled from 9 to 39 vehicles since April and is expanding into a dozen states by year-end — but investors want to see revenue, not just press releases, before pushing the stock back toward $500.

items.headline: Tesla Drops 4.75% as Trump-Xi Beijing Summit Ends Without China FSD Approval
items.summary: Tesla shares slumped Friday after a three-day Trump-Xi summit in Beijing ended without the regulatory greenlight for Full Self-Driving in China that the market had effectively priced in. Elon Musk had even joined the delegation on Air Force One. China FSD approval matters because it would let Tesla charge subscription fees against rivals BYD, Xiaomi, and Huawei that bundle similar features for free. With no breakthrough, the 'China premium' that had built into the stock unwound in a single session.

items.headline: Tesla CFO Confirms Negative Free Cash Flow For Rest of 2026 on $25B Capex Plan
items.summary: CFO Vaibhav Taneja told investors free cash flow will be negative through the remaining quarters of 2026 because of a record $25 billion capital spending plan — roughly triple Tesla's 2025 budget. The money is going into Cybercab production lines, Optimus humanoid robots, and AI data centres. Wall Street still projects Tesla ends 2026 with about $22.5 billion in net cash, so it can afford the burn — but two years of negative cash flow makes the valuation harder to defend if the AI bets disappoint.

items.headline: Robotaxi Fleet Hits 39 Vehicles; Tesla Targets ~12 US States by Year-End
items.summary: Tesla's unsupervised robotaxi service has scaled from 9 vehicles in early April to 39 by mid-May, with Dallas and Houston now live alongside Austin. Phoenix, Miami, Orlando, Tampa, and Las Vegas are being prepared next. The Motley Fool reports the goal is roughly a dozen US states by year-end. Musk also confirmed FSD v15 — the version needed for 'very large-scale unsupervised' driving — will arrive by early 2027, resetting earlier expectations of a 2026 ramp.

items.headline: Tesla Recalls 218,868 Vehicles Over Rearview-Camera Software Delay
items.summary: NHTSA forced a recall of 218,868 Model 3, Y, S, and X vehicles after Tesla identified a software bug that could delay the rearview camera image by up to 11 seconds when reversing — a federal safety violation. Crucially, Tesla says 99.92% of affected vehicles already received the over-the-air fix, and no accidents or injuries have been reported. The impact is more reputational than financial, but it lands at an awkward time as Tesla pushes regulators worldwide to trust its self-driving software.

items.headline: RBC: FSD Subscriptions Up 51% YoY, Europe and China Approvals Eyed in Q2-Q3
items.summary: RBC Capital reiterated Outperform after noting Full Self-Driving subscriptions grew 51% year-on-year in Q1 2026. The bank expects regulatory approvals for FSD in parts of Europe and China during Q2 and Q3 2026, and highlighted Belgium's Flanders region just granting Tesla approval to begin road-testing FSD. Subscription growth is high-margin recurring revenue — exactly the kind that investors want to see funding the $25 billion capex bill.

analysis_7d.summary: Tesla had a tale-of-two-halves week: a steady climb from around $398 to a Wednesday high of $445 fuelled by China-summit optimism, then a violent Friday reversal that erased most of those gains. The good news is the long-term setup is still intact — Tesla is sitting above its 200-day average and the Golden Cross is still in place. The not-so-good news is that price has slipped below the 5-day and 50-day averages, RSI has cooled to a flat 45, and MACD momentum is starting to roll over. Friday's drop happened on lighter-than-usual volume which suggests it was traders booking profits rather than mass panic selling — but next week needs to defend $418 (the 200-day line) or the short-term picture turns ugly.

May 03-08: Stock rallied from ~$400 toward $445 on robotaxi expansion headlines (fleet grew from 9 to 39 vehicles in six weeks)
May 12: Mid-week setback after operational glitches in Dallas/Houston robotaxi zones; recovered same week
May 15: -4.75% single-day drop after Trump-Xi summit ended without China FSD approval
analysis_30d.summary: Zooming out to the month, Tesla is up roughly 16% — a strong number that masks a very emotional ride. The story has been: Q1 earnings beat → robotaxi fleet quadruples → bulls extrapolate the China FSD approval that never came. Now the stock is consolidating between $418 (the 200-day average, a key floor) and $450 (the new ceiling). For the trend to stay healthy, Tesla needs to defend $418 next week; if it cracks, the next stop is probably the high-$390s. The fundamental story — FSD subscriptions +51% YoY, robotaxi cities multiplying — remains genuinely compelling, but the easy money on the rebound has been made and execution now has to take over from narrative.

outlook.recent_rating_change: RBC Capital reiterated Outperform on May 12, citing 51% YoY growth in FSD subscriptions and expected regulatory approvals in Europe and China over Q2-Q3 2026

catalysts.detail: Wall Street wants to see auto gross margin recover above 17% and proof that the $25B capex is translating into Cybercab/robotaxi revenue. A miss on either front could reset the AI premium quickly.
Approval would let Tesla charge for FSD subscriptions in the world's largest EV market — and would reverse Friday's selloff overnight. Rejection is now mostly priced in.

catalysts.timing: Phoenix, Miami, Orlando, Tampa, Las Vegas — rolling Q2-Q4 2026
catalysts.detail: Each new city is a tangible proof point that the autonomy thesis is real. But any safety incident in a new city would be a major setback to the narrative.

Musk says v15 is required for 'very large-scale unsupervised' driving. The slip from 2026 to 2027 has already reset expectations, so further delays would hurt — on-time delivery is the new bullish surprise.
70+ finished Cybercab units have been spotted at Giga Texas. Confirmation of high-volume production would validate the $25B capex plan and silence the cash-burn critics.

macro_factors.detail: The Beijing summit failed to deliver FSD approval and could herald a colder trade backdrop, which would raise battery costs and slow China sales
Markets are pricing in further rate cuts in H2 2026, which would lower the discount rate applied to Tesla's long-duration AI cash flows and help the valuation

macro_factors.factor: EV demand normalization in Europe and the US
macro_factors.detail: After two years of soft demand growth, Tesla's auto business needs price stability — any new round of cuts to clear the 50K+ inventory build would crimp margins

outlook.summary: The next three to six months will be defined by whether Tesla can convert narrative into numbers. The robotaxi fleet is real, FSD subscriptions are growing 51% YoY, and Cybercab production is visibly accelerating — but the analyst consensus price target of $405 sits below Friday's close of $422, meaning Wall Street as a whole thinks the stock has run a bit ahead of fundamentals. The wild dispersion between targets (Wedbush at $600, GLJ at $25) tells you this is a faith-based stock right now: bulls and bears are using the same data to reach opposite conclusions. The Q2 earnings report on July 22 is the next big tell. Until then, expect choppy trading in the $400-$450 zone unless either China FSD approval lands (bull case) or a robotaxi safety incident hits the wires (bear case).

recommendation.reasoning: The picture is genuinely mixed and that's why the call is HOLD rather than BUY or SELL. On the bullish side: the Golden Cross is intact, price is still above the 200-day average, RSI has reset to a healthy neutral, and the fundamental robotaxi/FSD story keeps adding proof points. On the bearish side: Friday's -4.75% drop pushed price below both the 5-day and 50-day moving averages, MACD momentum is rolling over, analyst consensus ($405 avg target) sits below the current price, and key catalysts (China FSD approval, Q2 earnings) are still weeks away. Confidence is Medium not High because both bull and bear scenarios are credible and could be resolved in either direction by a single headline. What would change the call: a decisive break above $450 with volume would flip to BUY; a break below $418 (the 200-day) would flip to SELL.
recommendation.key_risk: A failure to defend the $418 200-day moving average next week would mean both the medium and long-term uptrends are broken at the same time, which historically triggers a much sharper drawdown toward the high-$300s.
